text: Aside: When you have several things you want to say at the top level, we prefer it if you bundle them all up into one message. That way, when people reply in threads, the conversation can all stay in one thread rather than splintering into different threads for each top-level message. Here's our guideline about this: <https://futureofcoding.org/community#threading>

text: Now, in answer to your inquiry — one of the areas I'm particularly interested in is applying the technologies and metaphors we use for video games to the act of programming. That's going to eat up a lot of computational power doing things that seem superfluous (eg: making the code _look nice_), but it'll be in service of helping people better understand their systems. To this end, I spend a lot of time thinking about GUIs, so I'd be curious to hear more about what you mean by "get GUIs to be generable like queries".

text: "Where do people spend the most of their time in writing software?" In my job, seems like (1) reading code (including 3rd-party stuff with poor documentation), (2) debugging (including reading StackOverflow about error messages) (3) thinking about how to solve problems under the constraint of minimizing "risk" and changes to the zillion lines of code that are already written, (4) writing code to solve the problem, (5) refactoring code, (6) being distracted by more interesting things on the internet, (7) writing test code, (8) commit management - reviewing, writing commit messages, merging, rebasing, fiddling with task board, etc., (9) scrums/meetings, (10) searching for & reading documentation. (Disclaimer: just guessing, and reading code only comes first in low-quality codebases)

text: One idea I've been toying with recently is the idea that GUIs can be self-organising machines that assemble themselves based on rules. So you don't have to physically put components next to one another in HTML/JSX, they can just lay themselves out in the right places. I have a set of rules such as "leftOf", "rightOf", "above" that creates the layout
